summary: McKenzie provides a practical guide to on-page SEO tactics for small businesses, drawn from his direct experience ranking Bingo Card Creator on Google, Yahoo, and MSN. He covers six core techniques: unique keyword-rich page titles, keyword-optimized URLs, attention to pluralization and search term variations, consistent site navigation templates, and avoiding duplicate content penalties. The post demonstrates each tactic with specific ranking results from his own site, showing how small changes like word order in titles or dashes versus underscores in URLs produce measurable ranking improvements. He explains Google's 'supplemental index' penalty for duplicate content and offers a clever workaround: rewriting pages to be semantically identical but lexically different, exploiting the gap between human comprehension and algorithmic text matching.
key_points: Every page should have a unique, keyword-rich title with the most important keywords placed first, since search engines weight words at the beginning of titles more heavily
URL structure matters significantly for rankings — use dashes (not underscores) to separate words, and embed target keywords directly in the URL path
Search engines do not reliably perform stemming, so the difference between singular and plural forms of keywords can shift rankings dramatically — pick the variant with more search volume
Duplicate content triggers Google's supplemental index penalty, but pages can be rewritten to convey identical meaning with entirely different vocabulary to avoid detection
Consistent site-wide navigation templates help search engines strip boilerplate and focus on actual page content for ranking purposes
money_quotes: If your users and your desire to SEO every come into conflict, you have done something terribly wrong.
Going supplemental means your page gets quarantined out of the main search index for Google, and is put in basically a low-quality jail.
There is really no ghost in the machine, and some tasks require actual understanding rather than even the coolest applications of statistics.
The difference between SERP for 'cheap red widget' and 'cheap red widgets' is night-and-day.
How many interior pages do you have on your site which get read once in a blue moon? A simple change of their title to include some keywords and, bam, free traffic.
MSN is the world's cheapest date, who will practically faint if you show any interest in her at all.
key_insight: Small, systematic on-page optimizations — unique titles, keyword-rich URLs, precise pluralization choices, and non-duplicate content — compound into significant organic search traffic for small businesses without requiring any ethically questionable tactics.
